Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 128 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 4.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After pressing RIGHT:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 9-13 -> rows 19-23, cols 14-18 (moved right 5 cols)
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48 -> rows 54-58, cols 39-43 (moved left 5 cols)
So the right feet were NOT blocked from moving left at cols 44-48. They moved left to cols 39-43.
This confirms that the feet move in opposite directions and are not blocked from moving horizontally (within the body bounds).
Current state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 14-18
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 39-43
Initial state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
I'm back to the same state as before. The feet move in opposite directions, so I can't independently control them.
Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is not to restore the initial state. Let me think about what other configurations might be the goal.
Looking at the body shape, the left body extends from rows 9-58, cols 14-53 (the black shape). The left feet are at rows 19-23, cols 14-18, which is on the left side of the body. The right feet are at rows 54-58, cols 39-43, which is on the right side of the body.
Maybe the goal is to get the feet to the same row or column, or to specific positions on the body.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the body shape and see if there are any special positions.
The body is a black shape that looks like a person. The head is at rows 9-13, cols 24-48. The left arm is at rows 19-23, cols 14-23. The right arm is at rows 54-58, cols 39-53. The torso is at rows 24-53, cols 24-48.
Wait, but the body is a single connected black shape. Let me look at the segmentation to understand the body structure better.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the goal. Let me look at the initial image and see if there's a visual clue about what the goal is.
Looking at the initial image, the feet are at the "hands" of the body. The left foot is at the left hand (rows 19-23, cols 19-23), and the right foot is at the right hand (rows 54-58, cols 44-48).
Maybe the goal is to get the feet to the "head" of the body (rows 9-13, cols 24-48).
But the head is a single region, and there are two feet. Maybe the goal is to get both feet to the head.
Let me try moving the feet towards the head. The head is at rows 9-13, cols 24-48. The left feet are at rows 19-23, cols 14-18. The right feet are at rows 54-58, cols 39-43.
To move the left feet towards the head: press UP (to move up) and RIGHT (to move right).
To move the right feet towards the head: press UP (to move up) and LEFT (to move left).
But pressing UP moves both feet up, and pressing RIGHT moves the left feet right and the right feet left.
So pressing UP and RIGHT would move the left feet up and right, and the right feet up and left. This would move both feet towards the head!
Let me try this.
Actually, wait. I need to check if the feet can move up. Earlier I observed that the left feet at rows 19-23 were blocked from moving down, but I didn't check if they can move up.
Let me try pressing UP to see if the feet can move up.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 19-23, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 54-58, cols 39-43 (right)
# Let me try pressing UP to see if feet can move up

action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 14-18, cols 14-18
Feet: rows 49-53, cols 39-43
